mnemonics.title: DOT Product Pattern
mnemonics.content: D-DOT: D stands for Definition (sum of products), O for Orthogonal (equals zero), T for Triangle (used in triangle inequality). Remember: 'Do Orthogonal Triangles' - if perpendicular, dot = 0!

mnemonics.title: Cauchy-Schwarz Remember
mnemonics.content: C-S: C comes before S in alphabet, |u·v| ≤ ||u|| ||v|| - smaller (≤) comes before larger product. Think: 'Alphabetical Order = Bounded'.

mnemonics.title: Gram-Schmidt Steps
mnemonics.content: GS: Get First (normalize u1), Subtract projections from rest (u2 - proj + u3 - proj...), Keep going till done. Remember: 'Get Set, Subtract, Keep Going'.

mnemonics.title: Orthogonal Matrix Properties
mnemonics.content: QQT = I (Columns orthonormal), det = ±1, Q⁻¹ = Qᵀ. Remember: 'Quite Qtranspose Equals Identity'.

acronyms.expansion: Orthogonal Vectors Have Zero Inner Product - No Alignment
acronyms.usage: For vectors to be orthogonal, their inner product must equal zero. This helps remember the condition for perpendicularity.
Remember the inequality |u·v| ≤ ||u|| ||v|| bounds the dot product. Used in proving triangle inequality.

acronyms.expansion: Gram Schmidt Makes Independent Transformed Orthonormal
acronyms.usage: The process converts a set of linearly independent vectors into an orthogonal/orthonormal set spanning the same subspace.

acronyms.expansion: Non-negative, Zero only for zero, Homogeneous, Triangle

visualTips.visualization: Imagine light shining parallel to vector v onto u. The length of the shadow (projection) of u on v, multiplied by ||v||, equals u·v. Dot product = |u| × |v| × cos(θ).

In 2D: A line's orthogonal complement is another line perpendicular to it. In 3D: A plane's orthogonal complement is the line through origin perpendicular to that plane. W⊥ is always perpendicular to W.

First vector is the foundation (normalized). Each subsequent vector is built by taking the original and 'cutting off' parts pointing toward previous directions - like building walls perpendicular to the foundation.
Imagine walking from point A to C directly vs going via point B. Direct path (||u+v||) is always shorter than or equal to going via B (||u||+||v||). This is the shortest path principle.

analogies.analogy: Think of perpendicular streets in a city grid. They go in completely different directions - one doesn't contribute to the other. Their 'overlap' (dot product) is zero.
A unit vector is like a compass direction - it tells you the direction but has 'standard' length 1, like 1 mile or 1 kilometer as a reference unit.
Projection is like the shadow of vector u on vector v when light shines perpendicular to v. The shadow's length tells you how much of u goes in v's direction.
The norm of a vector is like the distance from the origin to the point. In 2D, it's the straight-line distance from (0,0) to the point - exactly like measuring distance on a map.
An orthogonal matrix is like a rotation or reflection that preserves distances and angles. It's a 'rigid motion' - it rotates or flips the space without stretching or distorting.

The dot product between two vectors is like the maximum possible when they point in the same direction, zero when perpendicular. It's bounded by their individual lengths - you can't have more alignment than the product of their lengths.
